title: VPC Building Blocks
description:
## The Amazon VPC

A **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)** is your logically isolated network in a Region. You define its IP space and carve it into subnets across Availability Zones. Almost every networking question at the Professional level builds on these primitives.

### Core components

| Component | What it does | Key facts |
|---|---|---|
| **CIDR block** | The VPC's private IP range, e.g. `10.0.0.0/16`. | Cannot overlap with networks you peer/connect to. You can add secondary CIDRs. |
| **Subnet** | A slice of the VPC CIDR bound to **one AZ**. | A subnet is **public** if its route table sends `0.0.0.0/0` to an Internet Gateway; otherwise **private**. |
| **Route table** | Rules deciding where traffic for a destination goes. | Each subnet is associated with exactly one route table. |
| **Internet Gateway (IGW)** | Enables **bidirectional** internet access for public subnets. | Highly available, horizontally scaled, attached to the VPC. |
| **NAT Gateway** | Lets **private** subnet instances reach the internet **outbound only**. | Lives in a **public** subnet; managed and AZ-scoped (deploy one per AZ for HA). IPv4. |
| **Security Group** | Instance-level virtual firewall. | **Stateful** — return traffic is automatically allowed. Allow rules only. |
| **Network ACL (NACL)** | Subnet-level firewall. | **Stateless** — you must allow both inbound and return traffic explicitly. Allow and deny rules, evaluated by rule number. |
| **VPC Endpoint** | Private access to AWS services without the internet. | **Gateway endpoint** (S3, DynamoDB — route-table entry, free). **Interface endpoint** (most other services — an ENI powered by PrivateLink, hourly + data cost). |

### Public vs. private subnet

```
Public subnet  : route 0.0.0.0/0 -> Internet Gateway   (inbound + outbound)
Private subnet : route 0.0.0.0/0 -> NAT Gateway         (outbound only)
```

### Stateful vs. stateless — the classic gotcha

> A **security group** is **stateful**: allow inbound on 443 and the response flows back automatically.
>
> A **NACL** is **stateless**: allowing inbound 443 is not enough — you must also allow the **ephemeral outbound** return ports (typically 1024-65535), or the response is dropped.

Keep these distinctions sharp; many SAP-C02 networking scenarios hinge on "why is return traffic being blocked?" or "how do these instances reach S3 without an internet path?"
